blurb: A rare penstemon cousin whose airy sprays of crimson tubular flowers look like tiny red birds perched in the branches — and pull in hummingbirds from June to frost. A long-lived, drought-tough New Mexico native for sun to part shade.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
seasons: Mound of green foliage spring–fall; crimson flower sprays June into fall; tan seed stalks after bloom
wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; also bees and other pollinators

care.hardiness: Cold-hardy across the Front Range — USDA zones 4–9, hardy to roughly 8,000 ft, so Boulder's ~5b–6a winters are no obstacle in well-drained ground.[1]
care.planting: Plant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost into average, well-drained soil; it accepts clay, loam, or sand. In containers use a fast-draining mix and avoid soggy winter pots, since potted crowns are far more prone to rot than plants in open ground.[1]
care.sun: Full sun gives the most flowers, but it tolerates part shade — and a little afternoon shade is welcome through Boulder's hottest July stretches.[1]
care.soil: Adaptable to clay, loam, or sandy soils of average fertility; the one firm requirement is good drainage, especially in winter, when wet soil rots the crown.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate. Water regularly the first season to build a deep root system; once established it is drought-tolerant, needing only occasional deep soakings in prolonged dry spells.[1]
care.spacing: Space about 18–24 in apart; mature plants form a clump roughly 2.5–3 ft tall and 1.5–2 ft wide.[1]
care.propagation: Grow from its fine seed, or divide established clumps; deadheading early keeps the plant vigorous and curbs self-sowing.[1]
care.bloom: Generous sprays of showy crimson, tubular hummingbird flowers from June through frost — one of the longest bloom seasons of any xeric perennial here. Deadhead early to boost vigor and prolong the show.[1]
care.feeding: Light feeding only — a single balanced, slow-release application in spring is plenty. Over-fertilizing pushes leafy growth at the expense of flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Low. Cut the stems back to about 4–6 in in mid-spring as new growth emerges, and mulch with gravel or coarse mulch to keep the roots cool and conserve moisture; avoid wet winter conditions.[1]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases reported; crown and root rot are the main risk in poorly drained or wet winter soil. It is not deer resistant, so protect it where deer browse.[1]
care.companions: A standout hummingbird plant for water-wise borders and rock gardens — combine with penstemons, salvias, agastaches, and ornamental grasses in lean, sunny ground.[1]

references.name: Plant Select — Scrophularia macrantha (Redbirds in a Tree)
New Mexico Rare Plants — Scrophularia macrantha

edible.summary: A figwort grown strictly as an ornamental hummingbird plant — no documented edible use, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Scrophularia macrantha is grown only as an ornamental and pollinator plant; authoritative sources record no culinary use and no safe edible preparation for any part. Figworts (genus Scrophularia) have a history of external/topical folk-medicine use, not food use — treat this as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots.[1]
